passages.explanation: A few days later, Jesus returns to Capernaum and word gets around that he's home. So many gather that there's no room left, not even outside the door. He's preaching the word to them. Four men arrive carrying a paralysed man. Since they can't get to Jesus through the crowd, they make an opening in the roof above him, dig through it, and lower the mat the man is lying on. When Jesus sees their faith, he says to the paralysed man: son, your sins are forgiven. Some teachers of the law are sitting there, thinking: why does this fellow talk like that? He's blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone? Jesus knows what they're thinking: why are you thinking these things? Which is easier — to say your sins are forgiven, or to say get up, take your mat and walk? But I want you to know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins. He tells the man: get up, take your mat, and go home. The man gets up, takes his mat, and walks out in full view of them all. Everyone is amazed and praises God: we've never seen anything like this! The teachers' logic is actually correct — only God can forgive sins. That's exactly Jesus' point. The visible miracle proves the invisible authority.

Jesus goes out beside the lake. A large crowd comes to him, and he begins to teach them. As he walks along, he sees Levi son of Alphaeus sitting at the tax collector's booth. Follow me, Jesus tells him. Levi gets up and follows. While Jesus is eating at Levi's house, many tax collectors and sinners are eating with him and his disciples — for there are many who follow him. When the teachers of the law who are Pharisees see him eating with sinners and tax collectors, they ask his disciples: why does he eat with tax collectors and sinners? Jesus hears this and says: it's not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. I haven't come to call the righteous, but sinners. Jesus doesn't wait for Levi to clean up his life first. He calls him from the booth — the symbol of his compromised livelihood — and immediately sits down to eat with his associates. Table fellowship in that culture means acceptance and solidarity.

John's disciples and the Pharisees are fasting. Some people come and ask Jesus: how is it that John's disciples and the Pharisees' disciples are fasting, but yours are not? Jesus answers: how can the guests of the bridegroom fast while he's with them? They can't, as long as they have him with them. But the time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them, and on that day they'll fast. No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment — otherwise the new piece pulls away from the old, making the tear worse. No one pours new wine into old wineskins — the wine would burst the skins, and both wine and wineskins would be ruined. New wine goes into new wineskins. Jesus identifies himself as the bridegroom — a title the Old Testament reserves for God in his relationship with Israel. His presence makes fasting inappropriate. And what he's bringing can't be patched onto the old system — it requires entirely new containers.

One Sabbath Jesus is going through the grainfields, and his disciples begin to pick some heads of grain. The Pharisees challenge him: look, why are they doing what's unlawful on the Sabbath? He answers: haven't you read what David did when he and his companions were hungry and in need? In the days of Abiathar the high priest, he entered the house of God and ate the consecrated bread, which is lawful only for priests. And he gave some to his companions. Then Jesus says: the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. So the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath. Two claims in two sentences: the Sabbath exists to serve humanity, not the other way around. And Jesus has authority over it. The Pharisees have turned a gift into a burden. Jesus restores the original purpose.
